old_claim.note: Printed Standardized Textbooks is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: The scoped practice is a published museum/cabinet book, so print infrastructure is the direct medium that made the catalog reproducible and distributable.

source_shape.source_locator: Linda Hall Library says Calzolari wanted to publish a catalog, that there were no museum catalogs in print in 1580, and that Ferrante Imperato published his museum book in 1599.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies the key transition from unpublished inventories to printed museum/cabinet books.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A printed catalog should depend on printing infrastructure, not on standardized educational textbooks, because the source describes publication format rather than classroom text standardization.

ontology_before: Public Museum Catalogs depended on Printed Standardized Textbooks, conflating collection catalog publication with educational textbook standardization.
ontology_after: Printed Cabinet Catalogs depends directly on the printing press as the publication medium for museum/cabinet books.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: public_museum_catalogs now depends on printing_press and no longer directly depends on printed_standardized_textbooks.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to standardized educational museum textbooks rather than printed collection catalogs.
A source showed early printed cabinet catalogs specifically emerged from textbook standardization rather than print publication.
A separate educational museum textbook node were introduced and should keep the textbook dependency.
short_reason: Printed museum catalogs depend on print publication infrastructure, not standardized textbooks.
